summary: Gruber reviews the 2018 Retina MacBook Air, framing it against Apple's increasingly strained relationship with Intel. He notes Apple barely mentioned Intel at the launch event while lavishing attention on their own A12X chip in the iPad Pro, whose benchmarks embarrass the Air's Intel CPU. The new Air itself is praised as a meaningful update with a retina display, Touch ID, third-generation butterfly keyboard, and excellent battery life. Gruber argues it's the MacBook most people should buy, though the broader notebook lineup is a confusing mess of overlapping models at similar price points. He sees the Air as a harbinger of Apple's inevitable transition to custom silicon for Macs.
key_points: Apple deliberately minimized Intel's presence at the event — one slide, one mention — signaling deep frustration with the chip partner
The iPad Pro's A12X dramatically outperforms the MacBook Air's Intel chip, illustrating why Apple wants to control Mac silicon
The new Air debuts with the third-generation butterfly keyboard, effectively skipping the problematic earlier versions
Apple's consumer notebook lineup is a confusing mess with four overlapping models between $1,000-$1,500, but the new Air is the only good deal
The MacBook Pro without Touch Bar has no clear purpose — it's barely faster than the Air, costs more, weighs more, and lacks Touch ID
money_quotes: Apple is not going to throw Intel under the bus — they're taking an 'If you don't have anything nice to say, don't say anything at all' approach.

What we're seeing here is a double whammy. On the one side, Apple's custom silicon team is firing on all cylinders, delivering new A-series chips year after year with ever-more-incredible performance and efficiency. On the other side, Intel has missed deadlines, and what they have shipped often isn't impressive.
Even before they've moved away from Intel chips, Apple is boasting about the performance of their own custom silicon, not Intel's.
There's no reason they won't pay as much or more attention to the Mac's custom silicon when they switch from Intel to their own chip designs. It should be downright glorious.
Why? I hate picking a CPU. Putting cost aside, I never know what the right balance is between performance and battery life. These are the sort of decisions I want Apple to make.
The $200 difference between the $1,000 non-retina Air and new $1,200 retina Air is quite possibly the best $200 value you can spend in the Apple Store.
The new Air is exactly that: the MacBook most people should buy, and exactly the MacBook everyone has been asking Apple to make.
key_insight: The 2018 MacBook Air is the Mac everyone wanted, but its Intel-constrained performance next to Apple's own A12X chip makes the case that Apple's silicon future for the Mac isn't just inevitable — it's overdue.
